current_fact.fact_text: From May 4–8, 1942, the Battle of the Coral Sea became the first naval engagement in history where opposing ships never directly saw each other, fighting entirely via aircraft launched from carriers. On its final day, Japanese planes crippled and ultimately sank the USS Lexington, demonstrating how war had shifted into the air.

episode.title: Morning Drops #05 - Electric Shock
episode.description: An energizing episode with electrifying sounds to wake you up and get you moving. High energy tracks that deliver a jolt of musical electricity to start your day.

current_fact.match_reason: The high-voltage guitar energy and sense of speed reflect the frantic aerial combat and sudden strikes of a carrier battle fought beyond direct sight.

queue.fact_text: On May 8, 1902, Mount Pelée on the Caribbean island of Martinique erupted in one of the deadliest volcanic events of the 20th century, obliterating the town of Saint-Pierre in minutes. Superheated gas and ash rushed down the mountain at hurricane speeds, killing over 30,000 people with only a handful of survivors.

episode.title: Morning Drops #47 - The Rise Of Autotune
episode.description:
In this Morning Drop, Yasmin explores the explosive rise of Auto-Tune, a tool that quietly changed the sound of music without asking for attention. From fragile, imperfect recordings to voices shaped with precision, this episode follows the moment when correction became standard and sound became controlled.

Along the way, we discover the curious story of how it was invented, how it moved from the background of studios to the center of debate, and how a few artists turned it into something expressive rather than hidden.

Listen slowly, and let us know what you think about Autotune.
songs: Chet Baker – I Fall in Love Too Easily

queue.match_reason: Both the eruption and Auto-Tune mark sudden, transformative disruptions—one in nature, one in sound—reshaping landscapes and sparking controversy about destruction versus creation.

queue.fact_text: On May 8, 2019, 17-year-old Isabelle Holdaway in the UK became the first reported patient to be treated with genetically engineered bacteriophages for a drug‑resistant infection. Scientists edited viruses that prey on bacteria, turning them into bespoke microscopic allies against an otherwise untreatable disease.

episode.title: Morning Drops #42 - The Joy Of Learning
episode.description:
Hosted by Yasmin, this Morning Drop explores learning as a lifelong process, focusing on persistence, repetition, and the emotional landscape that comes with challenging oneself over time.

Through personal stories and scientific insights, the episode looks at how learning reshapes the brain, generates joy through effort, and why progress often arrives after confusion and frustration. It reflects on the so-called delayed pleasure effect, the role of dopamine in motivation, and how curiosity and learning support long-term wellbeing.

The episode also questions our cultural relationship with failure and invites a softer, more realistic approach to learning, one that accepts mistakes as part of growth and sees persistence as a quiet form of strength.

Mood: reflective, grounded, quietly hopeful
Music genre: alternative rock, hip hop, electronic, neo-soul, drill

A space to listen slowly and stay with the process.

queue.match_reason: A pioneering medical treatment born from years of research and trial mirrors the episode’s theme of long, effortful learning leading to breakthrough and hope.

queue.fact_text: On May 8, 1984, London officially opened the Thames Barrier, a vast movable flood defence across the river designed to protect the city from storm surges and rising tides. It is one of the largest movable flood barriers in the world, a kind of mechanical guardian that quietly holds back the sea.

episode.title: Morning Drops #50 - Postcards from Earth-London
episode.description:
Postcards from Earth-London is a Morning Drops walk through the city Gaia has called home for the past ten years, a place made of sound, memory, and late-night streets. 

Four tracks guide the journey: from iconic landmarks to quieter corners, from romance to reality checks, and back to the reason the city still feels alive: music.

queue.match_reason: Centered on London’s urban spirit, this episode pairs naturally with the Thames Barrier—an engineering icon that literally shapes the city’s relationship with water.

queue.fact_text: On May 8, 1842, the Versailles rail accident near Paris became one of the earliest major railway disasters, when a train derailed and the wooden carriages caught fire, killing between 52 and 200 people. The tragedy exposed how new technologies can outpace safety, prompting changes in rail travel and emergency response.

episode.title: Morning Drops #45 - It Can't Rain Forever
episode.description:
In this first broadcast of 2026, Gaia returns from the vivid landscapes of Morocco to open a space for reflection. This episode navigates the delicate transition between closing a significant chapter and stepping into the new year. It explores the concept of constancy amidst change, celebrating the grounding force of friendship and the creative sanctuary that is Sedna FM.

The musical selection mirrors this journey, moving from the orchestral depth of changing seasons into the golden warmth of psychedelic soul. It recalls memories of Swiss mountains and unexpected downpours, reminding us that shared optimism can weather any storm.

Take a moment to breathe in the new morning and listen deeply.

Mood: Reflective, Warm, Transitional, Hopeful
Music genre: Classic Rock, Psychedelic Soul, Pop Rock, Soft Rock

queue.match_reason: A reflective, hopeful set about weathering storms pairs with an early industrial tragedy, framing it as a moment where hard lessons reshaped how humans travel safely.

queue.fact_text: On May 8, 1941, the German Luftwaffe bombed Nottingham and Derby during the Blitz, bringing the terror of aerial warfare to England’s industrial heartland. In a single night, familiar streets became unrecognizable landscapes of fire and rubble, changing how civilians experienced the war.

episode.title: Morning Drops #17 - Steel City
episode.description: Gaia takes us on a short trip through Sheffield's sounds — from electronic textures to raw guitars and sharp storytelling. Four tracks, one city, different edges of the same story.
songs: The Human League - The Sound Of The Crowd

queue.match_reason: An episode about an English industrial city’s soundscape resonates with another industrial region’s wartime bombing, highlighting how cities carry layered histories of steel, factories, and resilience.

queue.fact_text: On May 8, 1945, what began as formal celebrations of Victory in Europe Day in Halifax, Nova Scotia, spiraled into the Halifax riot as thousands of servicemen and civilians looted and vandalized the city. The outburst revealed the volatile mix of relief, frustration, and poor planning at the end of a long war.

episode.title: Sedna FM #01 - November Bomb
episode.description: The first Family & Friends radio episode from Yasmin Sarbaoui and Gaia Parolini. Where it all began — a life soundtrack that launched Sedna FM into the universe. Raw, heartfelt, and full of the energy that defines everything that followed.
songs: Canserbero - De la vida como pelicula, tragedia, comedia y ficcion

queue.match_reason: The explosive, chaotic energy and mixed emotions of the riot mirror the episode’s raw, high-impact feel and its sense of a powerful, messy beginning.

queue.fact_text: On May 8, 1927, French aviators Charles Nungesser and François Coli took off in their white biplane L’Oiseau Blanc, attempting the first non‑stop flight from Paris to New York. They vanished over the Atlantic without a trace, leaving behind one of aviation history’s most enduring mysteries.

episode.title: Morning Drops #39 - Moving Meditation
episode.description: An episode inspired by how travel helps us see more clearly. Stepping into new places opens the mind, shifts perspective. The music moves through balearic warmth, boogie grooves, world and latin-folk influences, and touches of new wave — a selection built to follow the quiet changes that happen when you're out of your usual rhythm.
songs: España Circo Este - Dormo poco e sogno molto
Talking Heads - This Must Be the Place (Naive Melody)

queue.match_reason: A meditative travel-themed mix complements the romance and risk of a pioneering transatlantic flight that turned the ocean into an imagined sky road.

queue.fact_text: On May 8, 1541, Hernando de Soto reached the banks of the Mississippi River near present-day Walls, Mississippi, becoming one of the first Europeans documented to see it. To the Spanish, this vast waterway was the Río de Espíritu Santo, a mysterious artery of an unfamiliar continent.

episode.title: Morning Drops #11 - Mediterranean Boat
episode.description: Gaia invites you to embark on a musical voyage across the Mediterranean. Exploring music from different countries shared by people who call them home. A warm, sun-soaked journey through coastal sounds.
songs: Fabrizio De André - Crêuza de mä

queue.match_reason: A boat-centric, waterbound musical journey echoes the spirit of riverine exploration and the awe of encountering a massive, unknown waterway.

queue.fact_text: On May 8, 1608, the silver mine at Hilderston in West Lothian, Scotland, was reopened under crown control by mining engineer Bevis Bulmer after being nationalized. For a brief period, this hillside became a royal experiment in extracting wealth directly from the Earth.

episode.title: Morning Drops #28 - Under Scottish Skies
episode.description: Gaia shares a personal moment of transition — a shift in seasons, in rhythm, and in life. From this space of quiet change comes a musical journey through Scotland: not the usual names, but voices that speak from the edges. A personal ritual and a small act of curiosity: an invitation to notice what grounds us when everything moves.
songs: Paolo Nutini - Pencil Full of Lead

queue.match_reason: A Scotland-focused episode naturally pairs with a Scottish mining tale, connecting modern cultural voices with an older story of digging into the land for hidden silver.

queue.fact_text: On May 8, 1984, the Soviet Union declared it would boycott the Los Angeles Summer Olympics, citing security and political concerns; fourteen allied nations later joined. The move turned the world’s biggest sporting event into a stage for Cold War tension and absence.

episode.title: Sedna FM #05 - On The Go - Zero Zero
episode.description: An energetic mix for when you're on the move. Zero Zero captures that feeling of starting fresh, moving forward, and embracing the journey. Perfect for travel, walks, or just getting things done.
songs: Grace Jones - Pull up to the bumper
Easy star all star - Soul love
James Brown - Talking loud and saying nothing (remix)

queue.match_reason: An upbeat, movement-focused mix contrasts with a politically enforced stillness, highlighting the tension between sport as joyful motion and geopolitics as a brake.

queue.fact_text: On May 8, 1946, two Estonian schoolgirls, Aili Jõgi and Ageeda Paavel, blew up a Soviet war memorial in Tallinn in a bold act of teenage resistance to occupation. Their clandestine sabotage made them unlikely symbols of defiance against an empire.

episode.title: Morning Drops #49 - Rage Against The Machine
episode.description:
In this Morning Drop, Yasmin revisits the story of Rage Against the Machine — a band born in tension, shaped by conviction, and remembered for moments that blurred the line between stage and protest.
From early Los Angeles unrest to Wall Street performances, this episode moves through rupture, resistance, and legacy — carried by songs that still feel urgent today.
Listen closely.
songs: Rage Against the Machine - Killing in the Name
Rage Against the Machine - Without a Face
Rage Against the Machine - Guerrilla Radio
Rage Against the Machine - Sleep Now in the Fire

queue.match_reason: An episode steeped in political protest and defiance mirrors the girls’ explosive act of anti‑Soviet resistance, amplifying their courage in sonic form.

queue.fact_text: On May 8, 1963, South Vietnamese forces under President Ngô Đình Diệm opened fire on Buddhists in Huế who were defying a ban on flying the Buddhist flag during Vesak. The killings of nine civilians sparked the Buddhist crisis, a mass nonviolent movement that shook the regime’s legitimacy.

episode.title: Morning Drops #52- Think Again
episode.description:
In this Morning Drop, Yasmin explores the quiet art of changing your mind, guided by Adam Grant's book Think Again. From the beliefs we carry without questioning to the identities we build around what we think we know, this episode follows the slow, sometimes uncomfortable process of unlearning.

Along the way, we meet a Serbian trumpet player who had to let go of one dream to find another, a Tunisian oud master who challenged centuries of tradition from the inside, and a Japanese band who found freedom in playing for no one. The music moves from Balkan brass to Mediterranean stillness to psychedelic drift, tracing the shape of a mind opening up.

Listen slowly, and ask yourself: is there something you believe today that deserves a second look?

queue.match_reason: The episode’s focus on revisiting beliefs and challenging traditions parallels how the Buddhist crisis forced a nation and the world to reconsider power, religion, and protest.

queue.fact_text: On May 8, 453 BCE, the powerful house of Zhao defeated the rival house of Zhi at the Battle of Jinyang, ending a brutal siege that involved deliberately flooding a city. The victory reshaped the map of ancient China and hastened the breakup of the State of Jin into separate feudal powers.

episode.title: Morning Drops #25 - A Trip-Hop Journey
episode.description: Hosted by friend Lorenzo, a journey through the roots and evolution of trip-hop. From Bristol's early experiments with Massive Attack and Tricky, to DJ Shadow's cinematic sampling in California, and Thievery Corporation's global downtempo grooves in Washington D.C. — exploring how this genre blurred boundaries and reshaped electronic music.
songs: Tricky - Hell Is Round the Corner
DJ Shadow - Midnight in a Perfect World

queue.match_reason: The moody, cinematic scope of trip‑hop fits an ancient, water‑weaponized siege and the slow political reconfiguration that followed, like a historical film scored in shadows and echo.

queue.fact_text: On May 8, 1997, China Southern Airlines Flight 3456 crashed while trying to land in heavy rain at Shenzhen’s Bao'an International Airport, killing 35 people. The plane bounced, broke apart, and caught fire, highlighting how weather and visibility can turn the final moments of a routine flight into catastrophe.

episode.title: Morning Drops #14 - Slow Planet
episode.description: A pause. A quiet return to slower rhythms, sparked by a live set under the summer sky. A moment to breathe and settle into the week.

queue.match_reason: The tranquil, weightless downtempo textures offer a kind of sonic soft-landing when reflecting on an aviation disaster shaped by turbulence and loss of control.

queue.fact_text: On May 8, 1984, Corporal Denis Lortie stormed the Quebec National Assembly with a submachine gun, killing three and wounding 13 before being calmed and disarmed by Sergeant-at-Arms René Jalbert, who spoke with him for hours. Jalbert’s composed conversation in a moment of chaos saved many lives.

episode.title: Morning Drops #43 - Looking Back
episode.description:
In this first Morning Drop of 2026, Yasmin opens a space for quiet reflection on the nature of transitions. The episode traces a personal journey that began three years ago, when a sudden injury on New Year’s Day turned a desire for movement into a mandatory season of stillness.

Through the story of a broken knee and the slow process of relearning to walk, the narrative explores the difference between being strong and being aware. It touches on the vulnerability of the body, the unexpected joy found in daily progress, and the warmth that comes from finally allowing oneself to accept help. The musical selection mirrors this path of recovery, moving from the uncertainty of change to the steady rhythm of healing.
songs: Kelly Finnigan - I Don't Wanna Wait
Goran Bregović & Iggy Pop - This Is a Film

queue.match_reason: A reflective story about healing, patience, and accepting help resonates with Jalbert’s calm, human intervention to de‑escalate violence and guide a crisis toward survival.

queue.fact_text: On May 8, 1942, the German 11th Army launched Operation Trappenjagd (“Bustard Hunt”) on the Kerch Peninsula, using concentrated air power and surprise attacks to shatter Soviet bridgeheads in Crimea. In just days, the operation encircled and destroyed large Soviet forces, demonstrating how strategy and terrain can be ruthlessly exploited.

episode.title: Morning Drops #51 - The Dancefloor
episode.description:
For International Women’s Day, this Morning Drop turns its focus to women on the dancefloor, not as a trend, but as a cultural force.

Across five tracks, Gaia follows a line from disco closers to hip hop club classics, tracing how the dancefloor became a place where women could take space, build confidence, and create connection, sometimes with friends, sometimes alone, always with music at the centre.

This episode isn’t about pointing fingers. It’s a gentle reminder that spaces like the dancefloor still matter: places where people can express themselves, meet each other with respect, and feel part of something bigger than a night out.

queue.match_reason: A war operation named after a bird and choreographed like a grim maneuver contrasts with an episode about reclaiming space through dance, turning coordinated movement into empowerment instead of destruction.

queue.fact_text: On May 8, 1942, as part of the same global war, the Battle of the Coral Sea and the Kerch offensive unfolded simultaneously on opposite sides of the world—a reminder that World War II truly spanned the planet. In one week, oceans, peninsulas, and skies became interlinked theatres of a conflict stretching from the Pacific to Eastern Europe.

episode.title: Evening Flows #00 - Introduction to evening flows
episode.description:
Sedna FM introduces a new format: Evening Flow, where you can enjoy curated music sets from selectors, DJs, and artists.

Each mix is an invitation to drift beyond the expected. A space where hidden gems from personal archives come to light, where sound speaks its own language. There are no rules here, no patterns to follow. Just the steady unfolding of a mood, a texture, a journey shaped by feeling.

Let the music lead the way - enjoy the trip!

queue.match_reason: An open-form, boundary-free mix mirrors the global, multi‑front nature of a war that blurred borders and turned the whole planet into one connected, shifting theatre.

queue.fact_text: On May 8, 2025, cardinals meeting in conclave elected Robert Francis Prevost as Pope Leo XIV, making him the 267th leader of the Catholic Church. With a new papal name comes a carefully crafted identity, signaling how he intends to interpret a centuries‑old role for a changing world.

episode.title: Morning Drops #10 - The One With Many Names
episode.description: Yasmin guides us through the glamorous world of Ghost, the Swedish band that turned rock into ritual. From masked clergy to shifting identities, diving into the music and myth of one of the most theatrical acts of our time.

queue.match_reason: An episode about a band built around mock clergy and changing names playfully intersects with the very real ritual of choosing a papal name and identity within the Church.

queue.fact_text: On May 8, 1902, among the few survivors of Mount Pelée’s eruption was Louis-Auguste Cyparis, who lived through a blast that killed almost everyone around him because he was locked in a tiny, thick-walled prison cell. His improbable survival later became part of a touring sideshow, turning a disaster into a bizarre form of spectacle.

episode.title: Morning Drops #33 - Immerse
episode.description: An analogy between diving, one of Gaia's great passions, and the act of immersing in life itself. A reflection on what happens when we go below the surface: when we let ourselves be fully present. From moments of calm suspension to warm, rhythmic flow, the selection moves like water — fluid, organic, and alive. An invitation to slow down, listen deeper, and float.
songs: Pino D'Angiò - Un Po' d'Uva e un Liquore

queue.match_reason: An episode about immersion, confinement, and water-like flow offers a contemplative lens on a man surviving because he was sealed away, suspended in his own small ‘cave’ while the outside world dissolved.

queue.fact_text: On May 8, 1984—the same day as the Soviet Olympic boycott and the Thames Barrier’s opening—these divergent events showed a world where global spectacle, political absence, and quiet engineering all shaped the future. It was a day when what didn’t happen (athletes staying home) mattered as much as what did.

episode.title: Evening Flows #01 - Yasmin - Crescendo in the Medina
episode.description: The first Evening Flow episode comes from Yasmin. It starts deep in the medina, slow, mysterious, textured, and grows into a liberating dance across sub-Saharan landscapes. A journey through ambient soundscapes, Arabic melodies, Afro-Latin rhythms, and funky highlife grooves.
songs: David Visan & Carlos Campos - Crystal Heart
Evan Mars - Ya Zareef Al Tool
Dele Sosimi & Medlar - You No Fit Touch Am (Medlar Remix)

queue.match_reason: A slow-building global mix that crescendos into dance reflects a day layered with politics, sport, and infrastructure—a quiet start that ripples into wide-reaching consequences.

queue.fact_text: On May 8, 1942, while battles raged elsewhere, ordinary people across the world listened for news on crackling radios, following maps dotted with distant names like Kerch and the Coral Sea. For many, this was the first war experienced through real‑time broadcasts, with voices and music bridging the gap between home and front.

episode.title: Morning Drops #54 - Intentional Listening
episode.description:
Do you remember what it felt like to listen to music without a streaming platform?
In this Morning Drops episode, Gaia reflects on a recent experiment: stepping away from algorithmic listening and going back to an iPod. 

What starts as a small personal choice opens into a wider meditation on attention, presence, and what happens when music is no longer treated as something infinite, but as something we choose with care.

queue.match_reason: An episode about conscious, deliberate listening connects with the wartime practice of gathering around a radio, choosing to sit with each broadcast and song as a lifeline to the wider world.

published.fact_text: On May 8, 1970, the Beatles released their twelfth and final studio album, “Let It Be,” closing the chapter on a decade that had radically reshaped popular music and culture. The album emerged from turbulent recording sessions, yet its title track became an enduring hymn of acceptance and calm.

episode.title: Sedna FM #06 - Alters of an Ego
episode.description: What if you could step outside yourself and become someone freer, bolder, or completely unexpected? Exploring the artistry of musicians who craft alter-egos and parallel realities to experiment with new dimensions of their identity. It's not about hiding who they are — it's about expanding who they can be.
songs: The Fireman - Strawberries Oceans Ships Forest

published.match_reason: Features Paul McCartney and themes of artistic personas and evolution, echoing the Beatles’ final transformation and farewell.

published.fact_text: On May 8, 1978, Reinhold Messner and Peter Habeler reached the summit of Mount Everest without supplemental oxygen, something many scientists believed was physiologically impossible. Their climb pushed human endurance to the edge of what the body can tolerate at extreme altitude.

episode.title: Morning Drops #22 - Sicilians On The Dolomites
episode.description: Hosted by Yasmin, a musical journey inspired by the mountains. Joined by two special guests, exploring the tracks they've chosen, hearing the stories behind their picks, and discovering the personal memories each song brings to life — a blend of music, voices, and landscapes you can hear.
songs: Florian Pellissier Quintet - Coup de foudre à Thessalonique
Lucio Dalla & Francesco De Gregori - Ma come fanno i marinai

published.match_reason: A mountain-themed episode that mirrors the high-altitude challenge and contemplative vastness of Everest’s oxygen-free ascent.

published.fact_text: From May 4–8, 1942, the Battle of the Coral Sea became the first naval engagement in history where opposing ships never directly saw each other, fighting entirely via aircraft launched from carriers. On its final day, Japanese planes crippled and ultimately sank the USS Lexington, demonstrating how war had shifted into the air.

episode.title: Morning Drops #05 - Electric Shock
episode.description: An energizing episode with electrifying sounds to wake you up and get you moving. High energy tracks that deliver a jolt of musical electricity to start your day.

published.match_reason: The high-voltage guitar energy and sense of speed reflect the frantic aerial combat and sudden strikes of a carrier battle fought beyond direct sight.
